back_translation: When all the kings west of the Jordan — in the hill country, in the lowland, and along the coast of the Great Sea toward Lebanon — heard, namely, the Hittite, the Amorite, the Canaanite, the Perizzite, the Hivite, and the Jebusite,
they gathered together to fight with Joshua and with Israel — of one mouth (unanimously).
But the inhabitants of Gibeon heard what Joshua had done to Jericho and to Ai,
and they themselves also acted with cunning. They went and disguised themselves as envoys, and took worn-out sacks for their donkeys, and old wineskins, cracked and mended,
and old patched sandals on their feet, and old garments on themselves; and all the bread of their provision was dry and crumbly/moldy.
They went to Joshua at the camp of Gilgal and said to him and to the men of Israel, 'From a distant land we have come; now therefore make a treaty with us.'
The men of Israel said to the Hivite, 'Perhaps you dwell in our midst. How then can I make a treaty with you?'
They said to Joshua, 'We are your servants.' Joshua said to them, 'Who are you and from where do you come?'
They said to him, 'From a very distant land your servants have come, because of the name of the LORD your God, for we have heard the report of Him and all that He did in Egypt,
and all that He did to the two kings of the Amorites who were beyond the Jordan — to Sihon king of Heshbon, and to Og king of Bashan who was in Ashtaroth.

So our elders and all the inhabitants of our land said to us, "Take provisions for the journey in your hand and go to meet them, and say to them, 'We are your servants; now therefore make a treaty with us.'"
This bread of ours was warm when we provisioned ourselves with it from our houses on the day we came out to come to you. But now, look, it is dry and crumbly.
And these wineskins, which we filled new — look, they are cracked. And these clothes of ours and our sandals are worn out from the very long journey.'
The men took some of their provisions, but they did not ask the mouth of the LORD.
And Joshua made peace with them and cut a treaty with them to let them live, and the leaders of the congregation swore an oath to them.
It came to pass at the end of three days after they had made the treaty with them, that they heard that they were neighbors and were dwelling in their midst.
The sons of Israel set out and came to their cities on the third day. Their cities were Gibeon, Chephirah, Beeroth, and Kiriath-jearim.
The sons of Israel did not strike them, because the leaders of the congregation had sworn to them by the LORD, the God of Israel. And the whole congregation grumbled against the leaders.
All the leaders said to the whole congregation, 'We have sworn to them by the LORD, the God of Israel, and now we cannot touch them.
This we will do to them: keep them alive, so that wrath will not be upon us because of the oath which we swore to them.'
The leaders said to them, 'Let them live,' and they became woodcutters and water-drawers for the whole congregation, as the leaders had spoken to them.
Then Joshua called for them and spoke to them, saying, 'Why did you deceive us, saying, "We are very far from you," when you dwell in our midst?
Now therefore you are cursed; and from you a slave shall not be cut off — woodcutters and water-drawers for the house of my God.'

They answered Joshua and said, 'Because it was clearly told to your servants what the LORD your God commanded Moses His servant — to give you all the land and to destroy all the inhabitants of the land from before you. So we greatly feared for our lives because of you, and we did this thing.
And now, behold, we are in your hand. According to what is good and right in your eyes to do to us, do.'
And he did to them thus, and he delivered them from the hand of the sons of Israel, and they did not kill them.
And Joshua appointed them on that day woodcutters and water-drawers for the congregation, and for the altar of the LORD, to this day, at the place which He would choose.
